questionText: The curve $y = x^{2} - 4\ln x$ is defined for $x > 0$. Find the exact coordinates of the point on the curve where the tangent is horizontal.

steps.explanation: A horizontal tangent has gradient zero, so we differentiate and set the derivative equal to zero.
The power rule gives $2x$, and $\ln x$ has the standard derivative $\tfrac{1}{x}$ for $x>0$.
The constant multiple $4$ carries onto $\tfrac{1}{x}$, giving $\tfrac{4}{x}$, subtracted from $2x$.

steps.description: Set the derivative equal to zero
steps.explanation: This locates the point where the tangent is horizontal.
Multiplying every term by $x$ (valid since $x>0$) clears the fraction and gives a simple quadratic in $x$.
Dividing by $2$ and square-rooting gives two candidate values.
The curve is only defined for $x>0$ (because of $\ln x$), so $x=-\sqrt{2}$ is not allowed. Keep $x=\sqrt{2}$.

steps.description: Simplify the logarithm at x = sqrt(2)
steps.explanation: Using $\sqrt{2}=2^{1/2}$ and the power law of logs brings the exponent to the front.
Since $(\sqrt{2})^{2}=2$ and $4\cdot\tfrac{1}{2}=2$, the exact height is $2-2\ln 2$.
This is the exact point on $y=x^{2}-4\ln x$ where the tangent is horizontal.
